(August 8, 1812 – May 5, 1882) was an admiral in the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"). He began his naval career as a commander in the American Civil War and during his postwar service became an admiral.

American admiral (1812–1882)

**John Rodgers** (August 8, 1812 – May 5, 1882) was an admiral in the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"). He began his naval career as a commander in the American Civil War and during his postwar service became an admiral.

## Early life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(admiral)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and career")]

Rodgers, a son of the famous Commodore [John Rodgers](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(1772%E2%80%931838) "John Rodgers (1772–1838)"), was born near [Havre de Grace, Maryland](/wiki/Havre_de_Grace,_Maryland "Havre de Grace, Maryland"). He received his appointment as a midshipman in the [Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") on April 18, 1828. Service in the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") on board [*Constellation*](/wiki/USS_Constellation_(1797) "USS Constellation (1797)") and [*Concord*](/wiki/USS_Concord_(1828) "USS Concord (1828)") opened his long career of distinguished service, and he commanded an expedition of Naval Infantry and Marines in Florida during the [Seminole Wars](/wiki/Seminole_Wars "Seminole Wars"). In the mid-1850s he succeeded Commander [Ringgold](/wiki/Cadwalader_Ringgold "Cadwalader Ringgold") in command of the [North Pacific Exploring and Surveying Expedition](/wiki/North_Pacific_Exploring_and_Surveying_Expedition "North Pacific Exploring and Surveying Expedition"), which added greatly to the knowledge of far eastern and northern waters.[[1]](#cite_note-Quanchi-1) Following his promotion to commander in 1855, he married and settled to work in the Navy's Japan Office in Washington, D.C., where he was serving when the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") broke out.

## Civil War service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(admiral)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Civil War service")]

Commander Rodgers' first war assignment was to go with Commodore [Louis M. Goldsborough](/wiki/Louis_M._Goldsborough "Louis M. Goldsborough") to [Gosport Navy Yard](/wiki/Gosport_Navy_Yard "Gosport Navy Yard") on April 20, 1861, where with other officers he was to remove Naval vessels and assets so they could not be used by the Confederates. Virginia had only just declared her secession from the Union. Upon arrival they found the yard in shambles, as [Commodore McCauley](/wiki/Charles_Stewart_McCauley "Charles Stewart McCauley") had already ordered the vessels at Gosport scuttled, including the [*Merrimack*](/wiki/USS_Merrimack_(1855) "USS Merrimack (1855)"), since he considered the yard indefensible. Commodore Goldsborough made the decision to destroy the yard, and Commander Rodgers and Army Captain of Engineers [Horatio G. Wright](/wiki/Horatio_G._Wright "Horatio G. Wright") were given the job of destroying the drydock. They were thwarted in this attempt when the fuse was extinguished by water in the pumping gallery. Commander Rodgers and Captain Wright were captured by General [William B. Taliaferro](/wiki/William_B._Taliaferro "William B. Taliaferro") of the [Virginia State Militia](/w/index.php?title=Virginia_State_Militia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Virginia State Militia (page does not exist)"), but since Virginia had not yet joined the [Confederate States](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"), and was therefore not at war against the United States, Governor [John Letcher](/wiki/John_Letcher "John Letcher") returned the two officers to Washington.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/John_Rodgers_naval_officer%2C_Civil_War_-_Brady-Handy.jpg/250px-John_Rodgers_naval_officer%2C_Civil_War_-_Brady-Handy.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Rodgers_naval_officer,_Civil_War_-_Brady-Handy.jpg)

John Rodgers as rear admiral

Commander Rodgers was then sent to the Western Rivers, where he organized the [Western Flotilla](/wiki/Western_Flotilla "Western Flotilla") and supervised construction of the [City-class gunboats](/wiki/City-class_ironclad "City-class ironclad"), the first ironclad [gunboats](/wiki/Gunboat "Gunboat") on the western rivers. He was relieved by Captain [Andrew Hull Foote](/wiki/Andrew_Hull_Foote "Andrew Hull Foote"), a more senior officer being required by the Navy to deal with the prickly Major General [John C. Fremont](/wiki/John_C._Fremont "John C. Fremont"). After blockading operations off of [Savannah](/wiki/Savannah,_Georgia "Savannah, Georgia") in command of the [*Flag*](/wiki/USS_Flag "USS Flag"), he assumed command of the experimental ironclad [*Galena*](/wiki/USS_Galena_(1862) "USS Galena (1862)") in April 1862, operating with distinction in the [James River](/wiki/James_River_(Virginia) "James River (Virginia)"). He commanded the [James River Flotilla](/wiki/James_River_Flotilla "James River Flotilla"), including the USS *Galena*, the ironclad [*Monitor*](/wiki/USS_Monitor "USS Monitor"), and the 90-day gunboat [*Aroostook*](/wiki/USS_Aroostook_(1861) "USS Aroostook (1861)") in an expedition up the James River in May 1862, which was stopped eight miles shy of [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_Virginia "Richmond, Virginia") by Confederate fortifications at [Drewry's Bluff](/wiki/Drewry%27s_Bluff "Drewry's Bluff"). The damage that the *Galena* suffered in the ensuing battle caused him to report, "We demonstrated that she is not shotproof", and made him disdainful of trying experiments in the fires of war. Thereafter he supported [General McClellan](/wiki/George_B._McClellan "George B. McClellan")'s [Peninsula Campaign](/wiki/Peninsula_Campaign "Peninsula Campaign") with Naval bombardment, preventing Confederate forces from overrunning the [Army of the Potomac](/wiki/Army_of_the_Potomac "Army of the Potomac")'s position.

John Rodgers was promoted to captain July 16, 1862 and took command of the ironclad monitor [*Weehawken*](/wiki/USS_Weehawken_(1862) "USS Weehawken (1862)"). After successfully navigating her from [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") to [Charleston](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina") through the same storm that sank the [USS *Monitor*](/wiki/USS_Monitor "USS Monitor"), he distinguished himself during the attack on [Fort Sumter](/wiki/Fort_Sumter "Fort Sumter") in May 1863, and in capturing the Confederate ram [*Atlanta*](/wiki/CSS_Atlanta "CSS Atlanta") on June 17, 1863. The latter service won him the [Thanks of Congress](/wiki/Thanks_of_Congress "Thanks of Congress") and promotion to commodore. Unfortunately, this was his last active service in the Civil War. After recovering from an illness, he took command of the ironclad monitor [*Dictator*](/wiki/USS_Dictator "USS Dictator"). Design and construction problems with that vessel kept him occupied for the remainder of the war, though he earnestly desired a more active post.

## Post-war career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(admiral)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Post-war career")]

See also: [Korean Expedition](/wiki/Korean_Expedition "Korean Expedition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Council_of_War_USS_Colorado_June_1871.jpg/330px-Council_of_War_USS_Colorado_June_1871.jpg)](/wiki/File:Council_of_War_USS_Colorado_June_1871.jpg)

[Rear Admiral](/wiki/Rear_Admiral_(United_States) "Rear Admiral (United States)") Rodgers as [Asiatic Squadron](/wiki/Asiatic_Squadron "Asiatic Squadron") commander, leaning over the table at right in this posed photograph of U.S. Navy officers holding a council of war aboard his [flagship](/wiki/Flagship "Flagship"), the [steam](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") [frigate](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") [USS *Colorado*](/wiki/USS_Colorado_(1856) "USS Colorado (1856)"), off [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea") in June 1871 prior to the [Korean Expedition](/wiki/United_States_expedition_to_Korea "United States expedition to Korea")

Rodgers then commanded the [Boston Navy Yard](/wiki/Boston_Navy_Yard "Boston Navy Yard") from 1866 until 1869. He was elevated to [rear admiral](/wiki/Rear_admiral "Rear admiral") in December 1869 and given command of the [Asiatic Squadron](/wiki/Asiatic_Squadron "Asiatic Squadron") on February 5, 1870. In 1871, he commanded the American squadron in the [United States expedition to Korea](/wiki/United_States_expedition_to_Korea "United States expedition to Korea"). Upon returning to the United States, he was selected for command of [Mare Island](/wiki/Mare_Island "Mare Island") on June 15, 1873, where he served for almost four years. He was appointed Superintendent of the [United States Naval Observatory](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Observatory "United States Naval Observatory") in April 1877 and died in the role in Washington, D.C., on May 5, 1882.[[2]](#cite_note-soley-2) He was buried at [Oak Hill Cemetery](/wiki/Oak_Hill_Cemetery_(Washington,_D.C.) "Oak Hill Cemetery (Washington, D.C.)") in Washington, D.C.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Namesake

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(admiral)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Namesake")]

Rodgers' father was Commodore [John Rodgers](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_War_of_1812) "John Rodgers (naval officer, War of 1812)") (1772–1838) and his grandfather, also named [John Rodgers](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(colonel)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Rodgers (colonel) (page does not exist)"), was a colonel who commanded a regiment during the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[2]](#cite_note-soley-2) Admiral Rodgers was the grandfather of pioneering Naval aviator Commander [John Rodgers](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_World_War_I) "John Rodgers (naval officer, World War I)") (1881–1926). Six ships of the United States Navy have been named in their honor; either as [USS *John Rodgers*](/wiki/USS_John_Rodgers "USS John Rodgers") or [USS *Rodgers*](/wiki/USS_Rodgers "USS Rodgers").

## Family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(admiral)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Family")]

He was the father of [William L. Rodgers](/wiki/William_L._Rodgers "William L. Rodgers"), who became an Vice Admiral in the U.S. Navy.

His brother, [Robert Smith Rodgers](/w/index.php?title=Robert_Smith_Rodgers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Robert Smith Rodgers (page does not exist)") [[Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q94273719#sitelinks-wikipedia "d:Special:EntityPage/Q94273719")], served as a colonel in the Union Army during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") and was married to Sarah C. Perry who was the daughter of Commodore [Matthew C. Perry](/wiki/Matthew_C._Perry "Matthew C. Perry"). His nephews [Frederick Rodgers](/wiki/Frederick_Rodgers "Frederick Rodgers") and [John Augustus Rodgers](/w/index.php?title=John_Augustus_Rodgers&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Augustus Rodgers (page does not exist)") [[Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q98304714#sitelinks-wikipedia "d:Special:EntityPage/Q98304714")] (1848–1933) (sons of Robert), would both become Rear Admirals in the United States Navy.
